title: Cat Over-Grooming: Why Is Your Cat Licking Its Shoulder Constantly?
meta_desc: Learn why cats lick their shoulders constantly, the urgency level for veterinary care, and how a photo helps with professional triage and diagnosis.
content_html:
Understanding Persistent Shoulder Licking

It can be worrying to see your feline friend focus all their grooming efforts on one specific spot. Persistent licking of the right shoulder often indicates localized discomfort, such as skin allergies, a flea bite, a joint injury, or even referred pain. This behavior is a cat's natural response to soothe an area that feels itchy, painful, or inflamed.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for localized over-grooming is Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening crisis, it should not be ignored for more than a day or two. Continuous licking can quickly break the skin, leading to painful secondary bacterial infections or 'hot spots.' You should schedule a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours. If you see active bleeding, pus, or if your cat is also hiding and refusing food, seek care more promptly.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, close-up photo of the affected shoulder is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. A high-quality image allows a professional to see the skin's condition—looking for redness, bumps, or hair loss—without the cat needing to be immediately restrained. These photos can help determine if the issue is a dermatological problem or something deeper, and they provide a baseline to monitor if the area is improving or worsening.

faqs.question: Can stress cause my cat to lick one specific spot?
faqs.answer: Yes, stress or anxiety can lead to psychogenic alopecia, where a cat grooms compulsively. However, a veterinarian must first rule out physical causes like pain or parasites.

faqs.question: Should I use an Elizabethan collar (cone) right now?
faqs.answer: A cone can be helpful to prevent further self-trauma to the shoulder until your veterinary appointment, but it will not treat the underlying cause of the irritation.

faqs.question: Could my cat have arthritis in their shoulder?
faqs.answer: It is possible. Cats often lick the skin directly over joints that are aching due to osteoarthritis or a soft tissue injury to try and soothe the internal pain.
